trial_name: MACMAN — Video Laryngoscopy vs Direct Laryngoscopy on Successful First-Pass Orotracheal Intubation Among ICU Patients (2017)
citation: Lascarrou JB, Boisrame-Helms J, Bailly A, et al; Clinical Research in Intensive Care and Sepsis (CRICS) Group. Video laryngoscopy vs direct laryngoscopy on successful first-pass orotracheal intubation among ICU patients: a randomized clinical trial. JAMA. 2017;317(5):483-493.
trial_type: RCT — multicenter, parallel-group, investigator-initiated, unblinded, superiority
one_liner: In ICU patients, first-attempt McGrath MAC videolaryngoscopy improved glottic view but did not increase first-pass intubation success compared with Macintosh direct laryngoscopy.

pico.population: Adults (≥18 years) requiring orotracheal intubation in the ICU for any indication.
pico.intervention: First-attempt McGrath MAC videolaryngoscope with indirect view on the screen, no stylet or bougie.
pico.comparison: First-attempt standard Macintosh direct laryngoscope, no stylet or bougie.
pico.outcome: Successful first-pass orotracheal intubation confirmed by waveform capnography.

background.existing_knowledge: Tracheal intubation in the ICU is frequent and physiologically hazardous, with high rates of hypoxaemia, cardiovascular collapse, and cardiac arrest. First-pass success is a plausible, modifiable driver of safety because repeated attempts increase duration of apnoea and airway trauma and may compound haemodynamic instability. Videolaryngoscopy improves glottic visualisation in operating theatres.
background.knowledge_gap: Effectiveness and safety of videolaryngoscopy in ICU intubations were uncertain given different case-mix (hypoxaemia, shock), operator mix, and urgency. Prior ICU data were dominated by observational studies and small trials, with inconsistent signals for first-pass success and limited harms reporting.
background.hypothesis: In adults requiring ICU orotracheal intubation, first-attempt use of a Macintosh-blade videolaryngoscope (McGrath MAC) would increase successful first-pass intubation from 65% to 80% (15% absolute improvement) compared with standard Macintosh direct laryngoscopy.

methods.design: Multicentre, parallel-group, investigator-initiated, unblinded randomised clinical trial.
methods.setting: 7 adult ICUs in France; enrolled May 2015 to January 2016.

inclusion: Adults (≥18 years) requiring orotracheal intubation in ICU for any indication.

methods.intervention: McGrath MAC videolaryngoscope (Covidien). First-pass technique: indirect view on the screen mandated for the first attempt. Adjuncts on first attempt: no endotracheal tube stylet and no gum elastic bougie. After first-attempt failure: repeat laryngoscopy or alternative airway technique at the operator’s discretion, consistent with local guidelines; subsequent laryngoscopy attempts could use videolaryngoscopy with indirect or direct view.
methods.comparison: Standard Macintosh direct laryngoscope. Adjuncts on first attempt: no endotracheal tube stylet and no gum elastic bougie. After first-attempt failure: repeat laryngoscopy or alternative airway technique at the operator’s discretion, consistent with local guidelines.
methods.blinding: Unblinded (operators and bedside staff); primary endpoint objective (capnography-confirmed), but open-label delivery could influence process measures and some complication reporting.
methods.sample_size_power: A total of 370 patients were required to detect a 15% absolute increase in first-pass success (from 65% to 80%) with 90% power at a 2-sided 5% significance level.
methods.primary_outcome: Successful first-pass orotracheal intubation (waveform capnography for ≥4 breaths).
secondary_outcomes: Any life-threatening complication (prespecified composite: severe hypoxaemia, severe cardiovascular collapse, cardiac arrest, death)
methods.follow_up: Peri-intubation outcomes during the procedure and up to 1 hour post-intubation; ICU outcomes followed to day 28.

headline_bullets: Videolaryngoscopy improved glottic view (Cormack–Lehane grade I 75.6% vs 52.5%) but did not improve first-pass success (67.7% vs 70.3%).
A post hoc severe life-threatening complication composite was higher with videolaryngoscopy (9.5% vs 2.8%), but this requires cautious interpretation.
Day-28 mortality was similar between groups (35.7% vs 36.4%).

outcomes_table.note: Success required waveform capnography for ≥4 breaths.
Better visualisation with videolaryngoscopy did not translate into higher first-pass success.
Measured from start of anaesthetic induction to capnography confirmation.
Composite included severe hypoxaemia, severe cardiovascular collapse, cardiac arrest, and death.
Post hoc severity categorisation; no multiplicity adjustment across secondary outcomes.
Follow-up outcome; trial not powered for mortality.

subgroup_table.note: Adjusting for MACOCHA >4 showed no difference in first-pass failure.
First-pass success remained similar in MACOCHA >4.

safety: Severe life-threatening complication (post hoc composite): 9.5% vs 2.8% (P=0.01).

domains.notes: Centralised, computer-generated randomisation using permuted blocks of 4, stratified by centre and operator status (expert vs non-expert). Analytic approach matched the stratified design.

Unblinded intervention and bedside outcome recording introduce potential performance and detection bias. Primary outcome was objective and pre-specified (capnography-confirmed). Many complications used physiologic thresholds, limiting subjectivity.
371 patients randomised. Primary endpoint missing in 5 participants; counted as first-pass failures per protocol. Per-protocol analysis included 365 participants.

Allocated device used for first attempt in 98.4% of both groups. Induction and preoxygenation were protocol-guided but clinician-selected; key elements were similar between groups. Mandated technique differences enhanced between-group separation.
Overall severity was high and balanced (SAPS II mean 58.0 vs 57.7; SOFA median 7 vs 7; PaO2:FiO2 median 95 vs 91). Indications for intubation were similar.

internal_validity.verdict: Internal validity is moderate-to-strong given robust randomisation, minimal attrition, and an objective primary endpoint; however, open-label delivery and extensive, unadjusted secondary comparisons (including a post hoc 'severe' composite) limit confidence in some secondary and harm inferences.

domains.notes: High-severity adult ICU population (SAPS II ~58; PaO2:FiO2 ~90–95), broadly reflective of many ICU intubations in high-income systems. Exclusions (e.g., 'no time for inclusion/randomisation', planned fibreoptic/nasotracheal intubation, pregnancy) reduce applicability to crash intubations and selected airway pathologies.

Device-specific (McGrath MAC) and technique-specific (indirect view mandated on first attempt; no stylet/bougie on first attempt), which may not map to current practice in some ICUs. Operator-mix (majority non-expert intubators) enhances relevance to training environments. Centres were in France with guideline-informed practice; transferability to systems with different drug availability, staffing, and airway pathways is uncertain.

external_validity.verdict: External validity is moderate: findings are most generalisable to routine ICU intubations in resourced centres using Macintosh-blade videolaryngoscopy with mixed-experience operators, but extrapolation to crash scenarios, other device designs, or different adjunct strategies is less secure.

strengths: Multicentre, pragmatic ICU trial with broad indications and high illness severity.
Central randomisation with stratification by centre and operator status.
Objective, clinically relevant primary endpoint (capnography-confirmed first-pass success).
High protocol delivery for first attempt (~98% received allocated device) and minimal missingness for the primary endpoint.
limitations: Unblinded design may influence operator behaviour and reporting of some complications.
Mandated technique (indirect view on first attempt; no stylet/bougie on first attempt) may not reflect optimised or contemporary videolaryngoscopy practice in all ICUs.
Control first-pass success was higher than expected (observed ~70% vs assumed 65%), reducing power to detect smaller benefits than the prespecified 15% absolute increase.
Secondary outcomes were not multiplicity-adjusted, and the 'severe life-threatening complications' signal was based on a post hoc composite definition.

critical_appraisal.summary: A well-conducted, pragmatic RCT with an objective primary endpoint that found no benefit of a specific videolaryngoscopy technique on first-pass success, though confidence in secondary harm signals is limited by unblinding and lack of multiplicity adjustment.
strengths: Objective primary endpoint with minimal missing data
weaknesses: Constrained technique may not reflect current practice

sections.authors_conclusion: Routine first-attempt McGrath MAC videolaryngoscopy (as delivered in this trial) did not improve first-pass success compared with Macintosh direct laryngoscopy. Improved visualisation alone is an insufficient surrogate for success in ICU intubation, where tube delivery, apnoea tolerance, and haemodynamic instability are dominant constraints.

clinical_bottom_line.verdict: This trial does not support routine first-line use of a Macintosh-blade videolaryngoscope with a constrained technique (no stylet/bougie) to improve first-pass success in ICU intubations. The decision to use videolaryngoscopy should be integrated with a physiology-first airway bundle and not based on laryngeal view alone.
clinical_bottom_line.applies_to: Routine ICU intubations in resourced centres with mixed-experience operators, using a Macintosh-blade videolaryngoscope.
clinical_bottom_line.does_not_mean: Videolaryngoscopy is harmful or has no role in ICU. The trial tested a specific device and technique; other devices, adjunct strategies, or patient populations may yield different results.
implementation_caveats: Device and technique matter: a 'see more, struggle to pass' phenomenon was observed, suggesting adjuncts like stylets or bougies may be critical for tube delivery.
Focus on patient-centred endpoints (first-pass success, complications), not just laryngeal view.
Implement alongside physiologically optimised airway bundles, minimised attempts, and structured training/supervision.

controversies.notes: The higher rate of 'severe life-threatening complications' with videolaryngoscopy (9.5% vs 2.8%; P=0.01) was derived from a post hoc severity categorisation, within a framework of multiple unadjusted secondary outcomes, increasing the probability of a chance finding and limiting causal inference.

Time-to-success was measured from start of anaesthetic induction rather than from laryngoscope insertion, potentially obscuring device-specific effects. Mandating indirect view and prohibiting a stylet/bougie on the first attempt was challenged as potentially suboptimal and not reflective of common videolaryngoscopy technique. The authors’ reply emphasised a pragmatic comparison with symmetrical adjunct restrictions.

context_related_trials.relation: A single-centre ICU trial that also found no difference in first-pass success (68.9% vs 65.8%; unadjusted P=0.68), aligning with MACMAN’s neutral primary outcome despite improved view with videolaryngoscopy.

A larger multicentre trial of critically ill adults where videolaryngoscopy improved first-attempt success (85.1% vs 70.8%; absolute risk difference 14.3 points; 95% CI 9.9 to 18.7) with similar severe complications, implying that context, operator factors, and device/technique implementation materially influence clinical effect.

unresolved_questions: Does a different videolaryngoscope blade design (e.g., hyperangulated) or routine use of a stylet/bougie improve first-pass success in ICU?
Is there a subgroup of ICU patients (e.g., predicted difficult airway) who benefit from first-line videolaryngoscopy?
What is the true safety signal for severe complications with videolaryngoscopy in ICU, independent of post hoc analyses?

quick_recall.one_line_takeaway: A better view doesn't guarantee a better pass; don't rely on videolaryngoscopy alone to solve a difficult ICU intubation without a plan for tube delivery and physiological optimisation.
